title: Wireshark capture — Windows update + cert revocation traffic from 192.168.157.134
html:
TL;DR

A Wireshark HTTP traffic view from a virtual lab machine (192.168.157.134 source — classic VMware NAT subnet, on a network forensics / malware analysis lab setup). The capture shows ~50+ HTTP GETs over a few minutes on 2025-06-06 at 00:43–00:47, hitting Microsoft, Google, Akamai, and Cloudflare endpoints for things like:

  • /ned.exe (suspicious — possibly the lab's planted malware sample or a Windows installer)
  • /msdownload/update/v3/static/trustedr/en/disallowedcertstl.cab and pinrulesstl.cab and authrootstl.cab (Windows trust list / cert revocation updates)
  • /r/r1.crl, /r/gsr1.crl, /r/r4.crl (GlobalSign / GTS root CRLs)
  • OCSP queries via long base64 paths to Microsoft / GlobalSign root chains
  • A Windows Store live tile preinstall query
  • Filestreaming service downloads from Microsoft (the filestreamingservice/files/... HEAD then GET pattern)

Why it's in the graph

This sits in Yahir's spring–summer 2025 OSU cluster alongside the mobile malware survey screenshot and the biometrics paper screenshots. June 6 capture, screen-grabbed June 10 at 1 a.m. — likely a homework assignment or write-up exhibit for a network/systems-security course (CS 372 / CS 419 / CS 466 territory).

The /ned.exe GET is the most interesting line — that's the kind of artifact you'd flag in a malware-traffic-analysis exercise. If this was assignment-driven, the rubric was almost certainly "identify the C2 callout among the noise of normal Windows update chatter."

What's actually on the page

  • Source IP 192.168.157.134 (VMware NAT default range — Windows guest VM).
  • Destinations: 142.250.217.67 (Google), 23.75.209.126 / 23.196.145.221 / 23.222.154.134 (Akamai), 104.18.20.226 (Cloudflare), 163.181.60.222, 172.253.117.94, 199.232.146.172 (Fastly).
  • Source ports climb 49859 → 50262 (ephemeral range).
  • Repeating Windows Update + cert revocation pattern is normal background noise.
  • The /ned.exe request is the anomaly worth investigating.

Loose ends

  • Confirm if this is OSU coursework or self-study — the screenshot doesn't have course branding, but the timing aligns with the rest of the spring-summer 2025 security cluster.
  • Worth tying back to the mobile malware / surveillance state concept cluster as another expression of Yahir's recurring systems-security curiosity.
